title: Multiband Superconductivity and Charge Density Wave in HfxZr1-xTe3 single crystals
authors: F. F. G. Nogueira, M. S. da Luz, M. S. Torikachvili, L. M. Ishikura, Ebrahim Dib, A. J. S. Machado
abstract: We report on the bulk multiband superconductivity and charge density wave (CDW) in HfxZr1-xTe3 single crystals. The parent compound ZrTe3 is a layered van der Waals material that undergoes a CDW transition near 63 K and exhibits only filamentary superconductivity below 2.0 K. Upon the introduction of a small amount of Hf (x = 0.02), the CDW transition temperature is reduced to TCDW ~ 53 K, while a robust bulk superconducting state emerges at Tc ~ 3.3 K, underscoring a subtle competition between CDW order and superconductivity in this quasi-one-dimensional system. Electrical resistivity, magnetic susceptibility, Hall effect, Seebeck coefficient, and specific heat measurements consistently confirm the bulk nature of the superconducting phase. The temperature dependence of the upper critical field Hc2(T) deviates markedly from single-band behavior, and it is well described by a two-band model, consistent with multiband superconductivity. Analysis of the Hall effect, and thermoelectric behavior reveal pronounced electronic anisotropy, with enhanced effective carrier masses, indicating that the subtle structural modification introduced by Hf substitutions affects the Fermi surface topology, as well as electronic correlations. Measurements of electrical resistivity in hydrostatic pressures up to ~ 2 GPa reveal that pressure drives TCDW to higher temperatures while suppressing Tc. These findings show that Hf doping can be used to fine-tune the balance between the CDW instability and superconductivity, possibly by means of chemical pressure effects, stabilizing a multiband superconducting state in Hf-doped ZrTe3.
summary: This work reports on the interplay between Charge Density Wave order and superconductivity in Hf-doped ZrTe3 single crystals. By applying chemical doping or hydrostatic pressure, the researchers demonstrate that these external parameters can tune the delicate balance between the two competing ground states. The findings confirm a multiband superconducting nature alongside strong electronic anisotropy.
main_problem: The study investigates the competition and coexistence between Charge Density Wave (CDW) order and superconductivity (SC) in layered van der Waals materials.
main_result: Hf doping or hydrostatic pressure can fine-tune this balance, showing that lattice compression reinforces the 1D CDW instability while suppressing the bulk superconducting state.
method: The research employs a combination of transport measurements (resistivity, Hall effect, Seebeck coefficient) and thermodynamic characterization (specific heat, magnetic susceptibility).
model_or_system: The physical system is HfxZr1-xTe3 single crystals, which are quasi-one-dimensional layered materials. The analysis involves fitting superconducting critical fields to two-band models.
key_observables: Superconducting transition temperature (Tc), CDW transition temperature (TCDW), upper critical field Hc2(T), and electronic anisotropy derived from Hall effect measurements.
important_parameters: Hf doping concentration (x=0.02, 0.13) and hydrostatic pressure up to 2 GPa are key tuning parameters.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that the observed phenomena can be explained by a competition between CDW gapping of the Fermi surface and multiband superconducting pairing mechanisms.
figures_summary: Figures show XRD patterns confirming phase purity, resistivity vs. temperature curves detailing SC and CDW transitions along different axes, and plots illustrating how TCDW increases while Tc decreases under pressure or with doping.
paper_structure: The paper systematically presents measurements on Hf-doped ZrTe3, first establishing the baseline competition between CDW and SC; then analyzing the effect of chemical substitution (Hf) to tune this balance; finally confirming the mechanism using external tuning parameters like hydrostatic pressure.
why_interesting: The clear demonstration of competing orders (CDW vs. SC) driven by subtle structural/electronic perturbations is a canonical problem in strongly correlated electron systems, providing experimental validation for theoretical models of electronic instabilities.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. -2X-ray diffraction pattern for the Hf0.02Zr0.98Te3 single-crystal showing only the (00l) reflections. The inset shows the Hf0.02Zr0.98Te3 crystal used for the electrical resistivity measurement with excitation current along the a-axis.

Figure 2. Temperature dependence of the electrical resistivity along the a-axis, ρa(T), for HfxZr1-xTe3 single crystals with Hf concentrations x = 0.02 (blue), 0.13 (black), and 0.25 (red), plotted on a logarithmic scale. Insets: (a) Low- temperature region showing the normalized resistivity ρₐ(T)/ρₐ, 5 K; (b) Temperature derivative of the resistivity, dρa/dT, highlighting the charge density wave transition. The CDW transition temperature, TCDW, was determined from the minimum in dρa/dT.

Figure 3. (a) Temperature dependence of the electrical resistivity for Hf0.02Zr0.98Te3 ρₐ(T) (left y-axis), and ρb(T) (right y-axis) over the temperature range 2–300 K. The CDW anomaly at TCDW ≈ 53 K and Tc, onset ≈ 3.3 K are indicated by arrows. The inset displays the normalized resistivity ρ(T)/ρ(5 K) in the low-temperature region (2–5 K), highlighting the superconducting transitions for both current directions; (b) Temperature dependence of the magnetic susceptibility in applied magnetic fields of 10 Oe (black dots), and 20 Oe (red dots). The divergence between the ZFC (zero-field- cooled) and FC (field-cooled) curves yields a T𝑐 ≈ 3.3 K for the 10 Oe applied magnetic field H // b-axis.

Figure 4. Temperature dependence of the normalized resistivity ρ(T)/ρ(4 K) for Hf₀.₀₂Zr₀.₉₈Te₃ in magnetic fields μ₀H = 0–0.3 T for (a) I // a and (b) I // b. The superconducting transition is progressively suppressed with increasing field. A small difference of the data for the two orientations of the field can be attributed to slight difference in the broadening. Insets show ρ(T) from 1.8 to 300 K. The upper critical field μ₀Hc2 was determined using a 50% criterion for the resistive transition.

Figure 5. (a) Isothermal magnetization curves M vs µ0H for Hf0.02Zr0.98Te3 in the 1.9 - 2.8 K range. The linearity at low fields is disrupted by vortex penetration at µ0Hc1; (b) Temperature dependence µ0Hc1 extracted from the magnetization data and fit within a single-band model. The extrapolated zero-temperature value obtained from the fit is μ₀Hc1(0) ≈ 0.22 mT.
